sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 54-year-old male presenting with a 2-3 month history of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheekbone area, which can be triggered by certain temperatures, wind, or cold.
The pain is described as excruciating and can last from a few minutes to an hour.
He has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and topical tiger balm without relief.
He was referred to our office by his family doctor for further evaluation.
Past medical history: The patient denies any history of multiple sclerosis or brain tumors.
He occasionally gets headaches, but they are not migraines and are managed with Tylenol.
Review of systems: The patient denies any sensory loss or numbness in the face, except for a slight numbness on the right side when the doctor's fingers are run along the side of his face.
He has not noticed any weakness on that side of the face.
Current medications: The patient is currently taking Tylenol for occasional headaches.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's smile is symmetric, and he can hear equally on both sides of his face.
The patient feels a little numb on the right side of his face when the doctor's fingers are run along the side of his face.
When the doctor pokes the patient's face with a needle, he feels a dull sensation on the left side and a sharp sensation on the right side.
Test Results: The patient's MRI shows a small blood vessel abutting and potentially pinching the trigeminal nerve.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has trigeminal neuralgia, a condition where compression of the trigeminal nerve causes shooting electrical pain in the face.
Problem list: Trigeminal neuralgia; active.
Plan:
I prescribed Tegretol 100mg PO BID for trigeminal neuralgia.
I will follow up with the patient in one month to assess the efficacy of the medication.
We will discuss surgical options if the medication does not provide adequate pain relief.
The patient will have genetic testing to ensure the safety of the medication.
Follow-up: The patient will return in one month to reassess the efficacy of the medication and discuss further treatment options.
